explanation: The input states: If the robot has a self-recovery strategy, it should explain it to the operator. In LTL, this is best captured using the implication: whenever 'selfRecoveryExists' becomes true (i.e., robot has a self-recovery strategy), then at some point in the future, 'explainRecoveryStrategy' should also hold. This is expressed as G(selfRecoveryExists -> F explainRecoveryStrategy). This formula ensures that at every point in time, if the robot has a self-recovery strategy, it will eventually explain it. Prior formulations that do not specify the causal relationship (e.g., F(explainRecoveryStrategy) or G(selfRecoveryExists -> explainRecoveryStrategy)) may not adequately capture the requirement that the explanation needs to come after or in response to the presence of the self-recovery strategy, and that the explanation does not have to happen immediately.

The natural language specifies that if the robot has a self-recovery strategy, it should explain this to the operator. This is an 'if...then...' conditional, which in LTL is captured globally as 'whenever selfRecoveryExists holds, explainRecoveryStrategy must also hold (at least at the same time)'. This matches the implication: G(selfRecoveryExists -> explainRecoveryStrategy). This is a direct and standard way to encode conditional statements in LTL. No previously given properties to compare against.

The natural language states that if the robot has a self recovery strategy, it should explain it to the operator. In LTL, this is expressed as a safety property: whenever 'selfRecoveryExists' is true, it should eventually be the case that 'explainRecoveryStrategy' becomes true (i.e., the explanation happens at some point after the strategy exists). This captures the conditional requirement that the explanation is only needed if the strategy exists.

The property states: whenever it is true that the robot has a self-recovery strategy available (selfRecoveryExists), it should eventually explain it to the operator (explainRecoveryStrategy). This directly captures the intent of the natural language: 'If the robot has a self recovery strategy, explain it to the operator.' It does so by using the LTL formula G(selfRecoveryExists -> F(explainRecoveryStrategy)), expressing that globally, whenever the condition holds, the explanation must follow at some point. Other LTL properties (for example, ones requiring immediate explanation with X or persistent explanation with G) would be either too strong or not necessary per the prompt, as 'eventually' is sufficient and faithful.

The given requirement states that if the robot has a self-recovery strategy (selfRecoveryExists), then it should explain this to the operator (explainRecoveryStrategy). In LTL, this conditional relationship ("if p then eventually q") is naturally captured with the formula G(selfRecoveryExists -> F(explainRecoveryStrategy)). This means that whenever 'selfRecoveryExists' becomes true, it is required that at some point in the future 'explainRecoveryStrategy' is true. This ensures that the explanation follows the recognition of the existence of a strategy. Previous LTL formulas that do not ensure this temporal ordering, such as selfRecoveryExists -> explainRecoveryStrategy without the temporal operator, would not capture the requirement, because they do not allow for the explanation to occur after the strategy is detected, nor do they repeat the requirement for multiple occurrences. The use of G (globally) ensures this pattern holds throughout the execution.

The input asks that if the robot has a self-recovery strategy, it should explain it to the operator. In LTL, this is a conditional obligation: whenever 'selfRecoveryExists' holds, then 'explainRecoveryStrategy' must eventually occur. This is best represented as a response property: globally, whenever 'selfRecoveryExists' is true, 'explainRecoveryStrategy' eventually becomes true. The resulting formula is: G(selfRecoveryExists -> F(explainRecoveryStrategy)). This ensures that every time the robot is equipped for self-recovery, it must (at some point) provide an explanation to the operator. Any formulas not using the response pattern (e.g., just 'selfRecoveryExists -> explainRecoveryStrategy') would not require explanation to ever occur or would not handle repeated scenarios, so the presented formula is appropriate and precise.

The input specifies that whenever the robot has a self-recovery strategy, it should explain it to the operator. In LTL, this means that globally, if 'selfRecoveryExists' is true at any time, then 'explainRecoveryStrategy' must also be true at that time (or possibly in the future, depending on strictness). The most direct and typical LTL formulation is 'G(selfRecoveryExists -> explainRecoveryStrategy)', which ensures that at every point in time, if the robot has a self-recovery strategy, it will also explain it to the operator at that time. This matches the input: there is no delay or requirement for eventual explanation—it's an immediate reaction every time the condition holds. Other LTL forms (such as using 'F' for eventual explanation) may not guarantee immediacy and thus would not fully capture the requirement as stated.

The input natural language states that if the robot has a self-recovery strategy, then it should explain this strategy to the operator. The correct LTL representation for this requirement is globally (G), whenever 'selfRecoveryExists' is true, then eventually (F) 'explainRecoveryStrategy' should hold. This is expressed as G(selfRecoveryExists -> F(explainRecoveryStrategy)), which means at all times, if the robot has a recovery strategy, then eventually the robot explains it to the operator. This captures the intended causality and timing of the statement.

The natural language input specifies that if the robot has a self-recovery strategy, it should explain this to the operator. This is a conditional requirement. In LTL, such conditions are typically mapped using the 'globally' (G) operator to mean that whenever the antecedent ('selfRecoveryExists') holds, the consequent ('explainRecoveryStrategy') should also hold. The implication (->) captures this dependency, and using G ensures the property holds at all points in time. Thus, the formula G(selfRecoveryExists -> explainRecoveryStrategy) correctly and succinctly captures the specification.

The natural language states that if the robot has a self recovery strategy, then it should explain it to the operator. This is a classic implication that should be satisfied: whenever 'selfRecoveryExists' holds, 'explainRecoveryStrategy' should eventually occur. The property 'G (selfRecoveryExists -> F explainRecoveryStrategy)' specifies that at every point in time, if 'selfRecoveryExists' holds, then at some point in the future (possibly immediately) 'explainRecoveryStrategy' will hold. No numerical operators are used; only the specified atomic propositions appear. This directly captures the required causality: the explanation is only required if the ability exists.
